senses.definition: the belief that truth and meaning depend on the perspective or context from which they are viewed
examples: Philosophers debated relativisms that challenged universal moral standards.
Relativisms in ethics suggest that different cultures may have valid but incompatible moral codes.

senses.definition: a person who believes that points of view are influenced by personal, cultural, or historical contexts and cannot be known to be true in an absolute sense
examples: A cultural relativist argues that practices vary across societies and should be understood in their own contexts.
As a relativist, she refused to claim one culture’s habits are universally right.

senses.definition: relating to or based on the theory of relativity or to the dependence of something on the observer or frame of reference
examples: Relativistic effects become noticeable at speeds close to the speed of light.
The relativistic view of time treats simultaneity as observer-dependent.

senses.definition: in a way that relates to or follows from the theory of relativity or in a way that depends on the observer's frame of reference
examples: Time dilation is thus viewed relativistically in high-speed regimes.
The data were interpreted relativistically, taking the observer’s perspective into account.

senses.definition: plural of relativist; people who hold relativist views about truth, morality, or knowledge
examples: Relativists often argue that there is no single standard of ethics applicable to all cultures.
Historically, many relativists challenging universal claims have influenced debates in anthropology.

senses.definition: the different states or aspects of being relative, or the instances of relativity; often used in physics or philosophy to denote aspects of relative timing, position, or value
examples: The relativities of time and space become evident in high-velocity experiments.
Abstract discussions often ignore the practical relativities of measurement.

senses.definition: a rarely used or nonstandard term referring to a person who emphasizes relativity in beliefs; not common in contemporary usage
examples: The term relativitist appears in a few obscure philosophical texts.
Some editors flagged relativitist as an unconventional coinage.

senses.definition: plural of relativitist; rare and not widely used in modern English
examples: A handful of relativitists debated the scope of relativity in ethics.

senses.definition: the quality of being relative or the theory that physical quantities depend on the observer; in physics, the theory of relativity
examples: Einstein's theory of relativity transformed our understanding of space and time.
In everyday life, even simple judgments can have a degree of relativity.

senses.definition: the process of making something relative or placing it in a relative context
examples: The relativization of values can complicate policy decisions.
Relativization of standards occurred as social norms shifted.

senses.definition: plural of relativization; acts or instances of making something relative
examples: The relativizations in the debate were aimed at reframing the issue.

senses.definition: to make something relative or to interpret something in relation to something else
examples: We must relativize moral judgments to different cultures.
Scientists relativize data to account for measurement biases.

senses.definition: made relative or interpreted in relation to something else
examples: The results were relativized to account for varying sample sizes.
Relativized explanations helped avoid overgeneralization.

senses.definition: makes something relative or interprets it in relation to something else
examples: The theory relativizes moral claims across cultures.
He relativizes time measurements to observer motion.

senses.definition: the act of making something relative or interpreting it in relation to something else
examples: Relativizing the data improved the fairness of the comparison.
They are relativizing standards to accommodate new evidence.

senses.definition: a person who relates or narrates; in linguistics, a word that denotes a relationship; in law, a party who relates a fact
examples: In legal texts, the relator may be a whistleblower who reports misconduct.
The relator provided a detailed account of events.

senses.definition: plural of relator; rare in everyday usage
examples: The relators in the document were questioned for accuracy.

senses.definition: a female relator; a rarely used gendered form in some technical terminologies
examples: The relatrix annotated the legal documents with notes.

senses.definition: a thing to which a relation is made; in mathematics or logic, a reference point or item
examples: In this equation, x is the relatum of the relation between variables.
The relatum of the discourse is the central claim.

senses.definition: to start something again after a pause or failure, such as a product, campaign, or service
examples: The company decided to relaunch the smartphone with new features.
They relaunched the website after a major revamp.

senses.definition: past tense of relaunch; started again after a pause or failure
examples: The product was relaunched to much fanfare.
After the outage, the service was relaunched with improved stability.

senses.definition: third person singular present of relaunch; starts again after a pause
examples: The company relaunches the product every two years.
New marketing efforts relaunches the campaign with updated messaging.

senses.definition: present participle of relaunch; starting again after a pause
examples: The company is relaunching its flagship app this quarter.
They are relaunching the service with a new pricing plan.

senses.definition: to launder again; to clean money or items a second time, sometimes used jokingly or in illicit contexts
examples: The auditors warned against relaundering funds through shell companies.
They accused the firm of relaundering proceeds.

senses.definition: past tense of relaunder; laundered again
examples: The funds were relaundered through a complex network.
Authorities traced the relaundered money to offshore accounts.
